known_projects.project: Cohort context intelligence layer running in TEE; this codebase.
Confidential compute network providing CVM (TEE) execution.
Reproducible TEE image building, packaged as a Make-OSI-based SDK.
Tool for building custom Debian spin-off ISOs; used by EZTE.
Reproducible TEE infrastructure project from Flashbots.
Articulation practice app with speech evaluation.
Biotech data-sharing platform for research collaboration.
Agent operating system based on a Debian fork.

known_technologies.tech: C++ runtime for running LLM models locally

example.chunk: [Hang] Yeah, we want to get rid of TPM. [Alex] OK, this also supports backwards compatibility for that. [Hang] Right, we should remove the TPM dependency from the GCP variant.

[Alex (flashbots?)] I will send you the link to the specific project. It's github.com/easy-te. [Shaw] easy te easy hyphen te, [Alex (flashbots?)] yeah, if you're forking tails, this will make your life a lot easier.
[Speaker 1] How does the attestation flow work when you have multiple nested enclaves? [Speaker 2] That's a good question — I don't think we've figured out the nested case yet.
[Shaw] Hang mentioned that Tina is working on the same problem space, you should sync with Andrew on it. [LSDan] Yeah I'll reach out to Andrew this week.

style_guide.kind: A course of action the group agreed on. Past or present tense ("we decided", "let's go with", "we should").
A concrete next step someone agreed to do. Often "I'll send", "you handle", "can you", "let me reach out".
A question raised in this chunk that is NOT answered within the same chunk.
A non-obvious observation or learning. Use sparingly — prefer a more specific kind when one fits.
A consequential statement that doesn't fit decision/action/question but matters for prep. Use rarely.

team_context.open_world_note: The lists above are NON-EXHAUSTIVE. New projects, technologies, people (including guests joining a single meeting), and topics WILL appear in transcripts and must be extracted faithfully even when not listed here. Treat the lists as ANCHORS for known entities, not as a closed vocabulary.
